common_name: Red Malabar Spinach (Red Vine Spinach, Malabar Nightshade Red)

planting.note: Direct sow or plant stem cuttings after last frost in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Red Malabar spinach is the red-stemmed form of Malabar spinach — a vining tropical Asian leafy green that produces abundantly through summer heat when true spinach (Spinacia oleracea) bolts and fails, with beautiful crimson stems contrasting the glossy dark-green leaves.

directions: Direct sow seeds or plant rooted stem cuttings in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; provide trellis or fence for the vining stems. Basella rubra (red Malabar spinach or red vine spinach) is the red-stemmed form of Malabar spinach, with strikingly beautiful deep-crimson to purplish-red stems and petioles contrasting with glossy, dark green, somewhat succulent, heart-shaped leaves. Botanically, red (B. rubra) and green (B. alba) Malabar spinach are sometimes treated as separate species, sometimes as forms of the same species — both are grown identically and used the same way. The ornamental value of red Malabar spinach is substantial: the crimson stems winding up a trellis create a dramatic tropical display throughout the warm season. The leaves are cooked like spinach — slightly mucilaginous (slippery) in texture, mild in flavor, wilting quickly in heat — used in stir-fries, soups, curries, and as a spinach substitute throughout South and Southeast Asia. In zone 9–12, it grows as a perennial vine; in colder zones, a summer annual. It is one of the very few spinach-type leafy greens that performs well in high heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast summer, bridging the gap when cool-season greens have failed. Stem cuttings root easily.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Malabar Spinach
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Leafy Greens

common_name: Wild Rocket (Sand Rocket, Annual Wall Rocket)

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–October in zone 9) in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; wild rocket is distinct from common arugula (Eruca sativa) with smaller, more deeply cut leaves, a sharper, more intensely peppery-bitter flavor, and faster reseeding — it is the traditional Italian 'selvatica' rocket used in authentic Italian mixed salads and Neapolitan pizza.

directions: Direct sow thinly in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; thin to 4–6 inches; harvest outer leaves continuously. Diplotaxis muralis (wild rocket or sand rocket) is a European native annual brassica closely related to but distinct from common cultivated arugula (Eruca sativa) — it is the species known in Italy as 'rucola selvatica' (wild rocket) and is considered by Italian chefs to produce a more authentic, intensely peppery-bitter arugula flavor than the cultivated form. Wild rocket has smaller, more deeply pinnately-lobed leaves, a more intensely pungent, sharp, mustardy-bitter flavor, and tiny, four-petaled yellow flowers (compared to arugula's cream-white flowers) — the flowers are also edible, mildly peppery and attractive as a salad garnish. This species tends to self-seed prolifically once established, naturalizing in disturbed, lean, dry soils where it forms self-perpetuating patches. In Italian cuisine, rucola selvatica is the traditional garnish for authentic Neapolitan pizza (layered fresh on the hot pizza immediately before serving), served with bresaola, aged cheeses, and in traditional Campanian and Pugliese salads. More bitter and pungent than cultivated arugula — use in smaller quantities or mix with milder greens.
sources: Italian Slow Food Foundation — Rucola Selvatica
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cool-Season Salad Greens
North Carolina State Extension — Brassica Salad Greens

common_name: Lemon Cucumber (Crystal Apple Cucumber, Round Yellow Cucumber)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Lemon cucumber (Crystal Apple) produces round to oval, tennis-ball-sized, pale-creamy-yellow fruits with exceptionally mild, sweet, non-bitter flavor — harvest when the skin is pale yellow to white; allowing it to turn deeper yellow indicates overripeness and seed development.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep in well-drained, fertile, moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after soil reaches 65°F; thin or transplant to 18–24 inches; harvest when pale yellow. Cucumis sativus 'Crystal Apple' (Lemon cucumber) is a heritage cucumber variety — one of the best-loved heirloom types — producing distinctive round to oval, tennis-ball-sized fruits with pale cream to pale yellow skin and white flesh that is exceptionally mild, sweet, and non-bitter compared to most standard cucumbers. Despite the name, the flavor is not lemony — it tastes like a very mild, sweet, refreshing cucumber. The thin skin makes peeling unnecessary, and the mild flavor means the seeds and seed cavity are also mild and not bitter, unlike some full-sized cucumbers. Lemon cucumbers are extremely popular with home gardeners for several reasons: the unusual round shape is attractive and different from grocery store varieties; the mild, non-bitter flavor appeals to people who find standard cucumbers too strong; and the fruits are easy to spot when harvesting (by size and color rather than hunting in dense vines). The variety produces prolifically over a long season. Best harvested young — large, deep-yellow specimens tend toward bitterness and seedness. An excellent choice for fresh eating, quick pickles, and cucumber water.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Heirloom Cucumber Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cucumber Production
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Lemon Cucumber

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Armenian cucumber is botanically a muskmelon (Cucumis melo) but used as a cucumber — it produces extraordinary long, pale green, lightly ridged fruits that can reach 3 feet, are almost completely seedless with a very thin skin and mild, delicate melon-cucumber flavor, and are extremely heat-tolerant and productive in hot Gulf Coast summers.

directions: Direct sow in warm, well-drained, moderately fertile, moist soil in full sun; provide trellis for best fruit shape; harvest at 12–18 inches for best texture. Cucumis melo var. flexuosus (Armenian cucumber or snake cucumber) is one of the most unusual cucurbits in cultivation — despite being called a 'cucumber,' it is botanically a muskmelon, not a true cucumber. The distinction matters in practice: Armenian cucumber is significantly more heat-tolerant and better-adapted to Gulf Coast summers than true cucumbers (Cucumis sativus), continuing to produce prolifically in summer heat that stresses and collapses standard cucumber vines. The extraordinarily long fruits (reaching 12–36 inches) are pale sage-green, lightly ridged, with a very thin, edible skin and white flesh that has a mild, slightly melon-scented cucumber flavor without bitterness. The seeds are nearly absent in young fruits, only becoming prominent in overripe specimens. Armenian cucumber does not need peeling, has no bitterness (the cucurbitacins that cause cucumber bitterness are absent), and is excellent fresh, in Middle Eastern salads (fattoush, tabbouleh), and in tzatziki. A standard fixture in Middle Eastern, Armenian, and Persian home gardens, it deserves much wider use in Southern gardens where true cucumbers struggle in summer.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cucurbit Production
North Carolina State Extension — Specialty Cucurbits

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–November in zone 8–9) in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; white lupine is an ancient Mediterranean grain and green manure legume — one of the oldest cultivated plants in Europe — grown for centuries as both a high-protein food crop and a nitrogen-fixing soil improvement cover crop; modern 'sweet' (low-alkaloid) varieties are preferred for both food and cover crop use.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in fall; incorporate as green manure at early flowering for maximum nitrogen benefit. Lupinus albus (white lupine or field lupin) is an ancient Mediterranean grain legume — one of the oldest cultivated plants in Europe, documented in Greek and Roman agriculture, long grown both as a protein-rich food grain and as a soil-improving green manure. White lupine is currently experiencing a significant agricultural revival as a European protein crop: the seeds are approximately 38–42% protein (comparable to soybean), 8–13% oil with favorable omega-3 fatty acid ratios, and contain significant dietary fiber. 'Sweet lupine' varieties with very low alkaloid content are used in Europe to produce lupine flour, lupine 'milk,' lupine tofu-analogue products, and as a high-protein additive in bread, pasta, and snack foods — an emerging alternative to soy protein that is rapidly growing in European food markets. As a cover crop in zone 6–10, white lupine is an outstanding cool-season nitrogen fixer and organic matter builder on poor, acidic, sandy soils where other legumes struggle; it has deep taproots that break up compacted subsoil. In zone 8–9, plant in fall for late-winter flowering; incorporate before flowering for the best nitrogen benefit.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cover Crops
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Cool-Season Legume Cover Crops

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, loamy soil in full sun; blue fenugreek is a distinct Mediterranean and Central Asian annual herb — the seeds are the essential flavoring ingredient of Schabziger (Swiss green cheese) and various Austrian Alpine cheeses, imparting a warm, herbaceous, hay-like, fenugreek-coumarin aroma different from and milder than common fenugreek (T. foenum-graecum).

directions: Direct sow seeds in early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; harvest seed heads when yellow-brown and dry seeds thoroughly. Trigonella caerulea (blue fenugreek or sweet trefoil) is a Mediterranean and Central Asian annual herb in the legume family — the specific herb used to flavor Schabziger, a distinctive small, green, very hard Swiss cheese also known as 'green cheese' or 'sap sago.' The ground seeds of T. caerulea are the source of the unique flavor of Schabziger — a warm, herbaceous, slightly fenugreek-coumarin aroma with hay-like notes, distinctly milder and less bitter than common fenugreek (T. foenum-graecum). The seeds contain coumarin and are also used to flavor breads, crackers, and Alpine herb mixtures in Austria, Switzerland, and Germany. The plant produces distinctive blue-purple flower heads somewhat resembling clover (hence 'blue fenugreek') and trifoliate clover-like leaves. The dried, ground seeds are available in specialty stores as 'Trigonella' or under the name 'blue fenugreek powder.' The fresh leaves are also edible — mildly herb-scented, usable as a green in salads and cooked dishes. Rarely grown in North American gardens but easily cultivated as a spring annual where fenugreek grows.
sources: Slow Food Foundation — Schabziger Cheese Ark of Taste
Swiss Federal Research Station for Agronomy
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Trigonella
